episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [TechCrunch] Nonprofit Current AI is racing to build the World Wide Web of AI, free for all and [Kottke.org] Ted Gioia : “Works like the Odyssey are..., plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Do cheaper production tools and streaming platforms fundamentally democratize rural filmmaking, or do they simply relocate gatekeeping to algorithms and attention economies? Topics include: Distribution gatekeeping: broadcast era vs. algorithmic era, Economic models vs. cultural value in streaming platforms, Owned vs. platform-dependent archives for local content, Skill training as infrastructure (IndigiNews model vs. individual gear access), Performances in the Park as a concrete test case for documentation.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Central Cariboo Arts & Culture Society

articles.title: [WIRED] This Luddite Puppet Hopes You’re Not Reading This on Your Smartphone
articles.author: Manisha Krishnan
articles.summary: On this week’s episode of The Big Interview podcast, WIRED’s senior culture editor Manisha Krishnan talks to Gowanus about eschewing Big Tech, going outside, and rejection in the age of dating apps.

discussion.central_question: Do cheaper production tools and streaming platforms fundamentally democratize rural filmmaking, or do they simply relocate gatekeeping to algorithms and attention economies?
discussion.riley_position: Streaming platforms have created new distribution pathways that don't require institutional backing, and rural creators can build regional audiences through owned archives and community channels without needing national algorithmic promotion.
riley_key_evidence: Connor Ratliff built a genuine following doing video essays directly to audience with no broadcaster backing
IndigiNews ReFocus program trains Indigenous photojournalists who distribute work under their own names on their own terms
Regional audiences can be reached through community screenings and local channels without national distribution
discussion.casey_position: Attention economy gatekeeping has simply moved from broadcasters to algorithms; rural creators now compete against infinite machine-generated content and broadcaster-backed films with marketing budgets they can't match.
casey_key_evidence: AI-generated songs from Suno flood feeds at volumes no individual human creator can match, drowning out small creators
Clement Virgo's 'Steal Away' gets algorithmic promotion partly because a broadcaster paid for visibility, not because algorithms are neutral arbiters
A two-hundred-person regional audience generates nearly zero revenue on streaming platforms that reward scale over specificity
discussion.resolution: They agreed that streaming platforms are useful as free hosting mechanisms but irrelevant as economic engines; the real gap isn't funding or gear but governance—whether communities own and control archives of their own cultural content, and whether training happens collectively rather than individually.
topics_covered: Distribution gatekeeping: broadcast era vs. algorithmic era
Economic models vs. cultural value in streaming platforms
Owned vs. platform-dependent archives for local content
Skill training as infrastructure (IndigiNews model vs. individual gear access)
Performances in the Park as a concrete test case for documentation
